text: play: Giacomo Casanova. Casanova and the Spread of Gambling in Europe: A Trickster at Play Tracing Casanova’s real-life story is not a simple task. He obsessively avoided entanglements, never married, kept no permanent home and had no legally acknowledged children. He was always on the move. The only memorial of gambling broke each of them: casinos are precisely liminal spaces that mimetically instigate our desire to win and possess something which does not in principle belong to us. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 161 Casanova anywhere in the world is a stone plaque on a wall of the minuscule laneway Calle Malipiero in the San Samuele district, declaring that Casanova was born here in 1725 to two actors. His father, Gaetano, was also a dancer. At the moment of his birth, Calle Malipiero bore an even more fitting name: Calle della Commedia. Luckily, Casanova decided to write down his own story, one that is so full of incredible episodes that it leaves the reader in utter disbelief. But as time goes by, more and more details have actually been confirmed by historians. Casanova is one of the most intriguing figures in modern European history. He met and discussed with eighteenth century figures like Voltaire, Catherine the Great, Benjamin Franklin and probably Mozart. He made his living as a gambler, an astrologer and spy – or as anything his vivid imagination could come up with. He was a restless writer. He translated The Iliad into Venetian dialect, wrote a science fiction novel, a proto-feminist pamphlet, and a range of treatises on mathematics, chemistry and music. He was also one of history’s most incredible travellers, criss- crossing Europe from Madrid to Moscow, and from London to Constantinople. He would intrigue everybody: kings, princes, writers and statesmen – not to mention the thousands of women he seduced. He wrote his famous Story of My Life in his penniless old age, while working as a librarian at the Castle Dux, in the mountains of Bohemia. That the figure of Casanova in some pertinent way speaks to modern lifestyles has been noted by a series of writers, and among them, Georg Simmel. Simmel invokes Casanova with particular emphasis in his essay The Adventure, In this essay, Simmel tries to identify the nature of experience: what characterizes it as a psychological phenomenon? What are the attributes of adventure as a category of experience? Simmel’s entire discussion here also seems strongly influenced by Kierkegaard, both in the singling out of a person connected to an activity which comes to stand for a ‘position’ (as in ‘the stranger’), and in the substantial discussion of the nature of experience, very much linked to the temporal dimension. Casanova is the seducer by whom all other seducers in modernity are measured, the pure sensualist. He stands for Simmel as the Weberian ideal-type of the man who only lives to seek pleasure, as does don Giovanni for Kierkegaard in Either/Or. Within the continuous flow of events that make up the arc of this existence, Simmel writes, there are experiences that seem discontinuous from the rest: moments of intensity cut off from the sensory experiences of the everyday, islands that rise up from the daily events that wash over us and circulate around us. At the same time, Simmel argues that every single experience contains a modicum of the characteristics which, if they grow beyond a certain point, bring it to the ‘threshold’ of adventure. To Simmel, Casanova is the archetypical example of an adventurer, because he lives in ‘pure presentness’: For this reason, the adventurer is also the extreme example of the ahistorical individual, of the man who lives in the present. On the one hand, he is not determined by any past (and this marks the contrast between him and the aged, Liminality and the Modern 162 of which more later); nor, on the other hand, does the future exist for him. An extraordinary characteristic proof of this is that Casanova (as may be seen from his memoirs), in the course of his erotic-adventurous life, every so often seriously intended to marry a woman with whom he was in love at the time. In the light of his temperament and conduct of life, we can imagine nothing more obviously impossible, internally and externally. Casanova not only had excellent knowledge of men but also rare knowledge of himself. Although he must have said to himself that he could not stand marriage even two weeks and that the most miserable consequences of such a step would be quite unavoidable, his perspective on the future was wholly obliterated in the rapture of the moment. (Saying this, I mean to put the emphasis on the moment rather than on the rapture.) Because he was entirely dominated by the feeling of the present, he wanted to enter into a future relationship which was impossible precisely because his temperament was oriented to the present. (Simmel, the Adventurer, section 6) Casanova and Life as a Trickster Casanova (1725–1798) was not only born to actors, he had himself ‘theatre in his blood, and the world became his stage’ (Childs 1989: 1). The Venice of his time was completely given over to the pursuit of pleasure. By the early eighteenth century, the city was crowded with theatres and spontaneous street acts. There was gambling and gaming everywhere, and carnival, where everyone went masked and played identity games, extended over half the year (ibid.). In many ways, Casanova was born into this world of permanent liminality, but he also had a role to play in the way it spread outside his native city. It is well-known that Casanova was thrown in prison one July night in 1755, just after his 30th birthday, when police burst into his bedroom. In a society whose excesses were alternately indulged and controlled, he had been singled out by the Venetian Inquisition’s spies for prosecution as, amongst other things, a cheater, magician, freemason and a blasphemer (possibly the length of this
